title: Somatic Experiencing: What Actually Happens in a Session
researcherTopic: C-PTSD, the four F types, emotional flashbacks
metaDescription: Discover what truly happens in a Somatic Experiencing session. Learn how this body-based approach helps heal trauma by working with the nervous system's wisdom.
imageDescription: Golden light filters through a window, illuminating the delicate, intersecting lines of a complex neural network diagram drawn on glass. The atmosphere is one of warmth, clarity, and profound insight into the body's inner workings.
htmlContent:
Pete Walker, in his extensive work on C-PTSD, gave us a map for the territory of emotional flashbacks and the complex internal spaces they create ~ a language for the experience of being hijacked by the past. Yet, even with this map, the question of how to work through that territory in the living, breathing moments of a therapeutic session remains one of the most vital and misunderstood aspects of healing. We can read all the books, understand the concepts, and still find ourselves utterly lost when the body takes over, because the work of Somatic Experiencing is not about knowing, but about listening to a conversation that has been happening just beneath the level of our conscious awareness for our entire lives.

The Architecture of Sensation

Before a word is ever spoken in a Somatic Experiencing session, a different kind of dialogue has already begun. It is the dialogue of posture, of breath, of the subtle shifts in muscle tension that signal a nervous system bracing for an impact that happened decades ago. In my years of working in this territory, I have seen countless individuals arrive with a perfectly coherent narrative of their trauma, yet their bodies are telling an entirely different story - a story of collapse, of freeze, of a desperate fight that was never completed. We are not excavating the past; we are attending to its living architecture in the present moment. The therapist’s attention is not on the content of the memory, but on the container of the body, tracking the subtle currents of activation and settling that reveal the path toward regulation. It is a process of learning to read the body's grammar, a language spoken not in words, but in sensation, temperature, and pressure.

This is not a passive process of simply feeling. It is an active, collaborative inquiry into the sensory world. The invitation is to notice, not to analyze. To track, not to interpret. We might start with a simple question: “As you speak about that, what do you notice happening inside?” The answer is not a thought. It is a felt shift... This is the entry point. This is the beginning of the thread that, when followed with gentle, unwavering attention, can lead the nervous system out of the labyrinth of the past. We are building the capacity for dual awareness: one foot in the present-moment sensory experience, and one foot in the story that wants to be told. It is in this delicate balance that the trauma narrative can finally be witnessed without overwhelming the system, allowing for a new, more integrated experience to emerge. We are not trying to get rid of the activation; we are trying to create enough space around it for it to move, to shift, and ultimately, to complete its interrupted path.

Pendulation: The Rhythm of Healing

One of the core principles of Somatic Experiencing is the concept of pendulation... the natural rhythm of moving between a state of activation or distress and a state of relative calm or resource. The traumatized nervous system gets stuck in one pole of this rhythm, perpetually frozen in a state of high alert or shutdown. A client once described this as learning to breathe again after having held her breath for twenty years. It is not about forcing a state of relaxation, but about discovering that a state of non-activation is also available, even if only for a moment. This discovery is not an intellectual one; it is a deeply felt, physiological experience that begins to retrain the nervous system’s expectations.

We might find this resource in the simple sensation of the feet on the floor, the feeling of the back against the chair, or a memory of a place where one felt a sense of ease. The goal is not to escape the discomfort, but to build a container strong enough to hold it. By intentionally guiding attention back and forth between the difficult sensations and the pleasant or neutral ones, we are doing something striking: we are teaching the nervous system that it can survive the intensity of the trauma activation. We are proving, in real time, that the feeling is not endless, that it has a beginning, a middle, and an end. For more on how the body stores these experiences, one might explore the article on the body's memory. This gentle oscillation is the engine of titration, the process of touching into the trauma just enough to create a small amount of activation, and then returning to resource to allow it to integrate. It is a slow, respectful process that honors the body’s intrinsic wisdom and its own timeline for healing.

A tool that many find helpful for releasing stored body tension is TriggerPoint GRID Foam Roller (paid link).

Titration and the Unfinished Response

Think about that for a second.

Discharge: The Return of Aliveness

As the frozen survival energy is allowed to thaw and move through the system, a process known as discharge can occur. What emerges is often misunderstood as the emotional catharsis mentioned earlier, but it is a physiological phenomenon, not an emotional one. It is the sign that the nervous system is releasing stored energy and returning to a state of regulation. Discharge can symptom in many ways: trembling, shaking, a sudden deep breath, a wave of heat or cold, or even tears. They are the body’s way of resetting itself, of completing the stress cycle. In a session, the therapist helps the client to stay present with these sensations without becoming alarmed, to allow the body’s natural healing mechanisms to do their work. It is a moment of deep trust in the body’s intelligence.

Integration and the New Narrative

The final phase of a Somatic Experiencing session, and indeed of the entire therapeutic process, is integration. Something changes when the shifts that have occurred on a physiological level begin to ripple out into one’s cognitive and emotional life. As the nervous system becomes more regulated, the world begins to look and feel different. The hypervigilance softens. There is more space between stimulus and response. The stories we tell ourselves about who we are and what happened to us begin to change, not because we are forcing a new narrative, but because the underlying neurobiology that supported the old narrative has been rewired. The trauma is no longer the organizing principle of one’s identity. It becomes a part of one’s history, but not the entirety of it.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger (paid link).

This integration is not a passive process. It is supported by the ongoing practice of bringing mindful attention to the shifts in one’s internal state, of noticing the new capacity for connection, for pleasure, for stillness. It is about learning to inhabit this new, more regulated nervous system. The work of the session creates the opening, but the work of life is to walk through it. In this territory, the insights from teachers like Pete Walker become not just intellectual concepts, but lived realities. We learn to meet these moments not with fear or self-recrimination, but with the same gentle, curious attention that we cultivated in the session. It turns out to be the ultimate gift of this work: the return of agency. We are no longer at the mercy of our history. We become the active participants in our own healing, co-creating a new reality with a nervous system that is finally, after so long, on our own side.

The Terrain of Your Experience

Ultimately, a Somatic Experiencing session is a microcosm of a larger process - the process of returning to oneself. It is a practice of embodied attention, a commitment to listening to the wisdom of the body, and a courageous willingness to meet the echoes of the past with presence. It is not a quick fix, nor is it a linear path. The session itself is not the magic. The magic is in the way it teaches us to pay attention, to trust the subtle currents of our own life force, and to reclaim the parts of ourselves that we thought were lost forever. It is a process that reminds us that healing is not about erasing the past, but about creating a present that is spacious enough to hold it.

The comfortable ending would be to tell you that everything will be fine. But that would be a lie dressed up as kindness. Do it anyway. The alternative is worse.

Frequently Asked Questions

What if I don't feel anything in a session?

It turns out to be a common and valid experience. Numbness or a sense of disconnection is often a protective strategy of the nervous system. A skilled practitioner will not force you to feel something, but will work with the numbness itself as a valid and important sensation, gently exploring its edges and creating the safety for feeling to return at its own pace.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link).

Not what you expected, right?

Is Somatic Experiencing the same as talk therapy?

No, it is quite different. While talking is involved, the primary focus is not on the cognitive narrative of your story, but on the physiological sensations and impulses that arise in your body. It is a "bottom-up" approach that works directly with the nervous system, rather than a "top-down" approach that focuses on thoughts and beliefs.

How long does it take to see results?

Healing is not a linear process and the timeline is unique for every individual. Some people experience subtle shifts in their sense of safety and regulation within a few sessions. For deep-seated complex trauma, the process is a longer-term path of gradually building nervous system capacity and resilience over time.

A body tool that some find helpful for releasing chronic tension is Acupressure Mat and Pillow Set (paid link).

Can this be done online?

Yes, Somatic Experiencing can be effectively facilitated online. A skilled practitioner is able to track the subtle physiological shifts through the screen and guide you through the process. The core principles of creating safety, tracking sensation, and working with titration and pendulation remain the same in a virtual setting.
